slides.title: Artemis II completed the first crewed Artemis lunar test flight
slides.subtitle: Apr 1 to Apr 10, 2026 | NASA official sources

slides.title: Artemis II made Artemis operational
slides.subtitle: NASA used the mission to prove the human-rated stack in real flight conditions
bullets: Artemis II was NASA's first crewed test flight in lunar space using SLS and Orion.
The crew completed a nearly 10-day mission around the Moon and back to Earth.
NASA described the mission as the first human trip to the Moon in more than half a century.
With the crew home, NASA shifted focus to assembling Artemis III.

slides.title: The crew blended command and partnership
slides.subtitle: Roles announced by NASA for the first Artemis crew

quadrants.title: Reid Wiseman
quadrants.body: Commander. Led mission execution and crew decisions aboard Orion.

quadrants.title: Victor Glover
quadrants.body: Pilot. Supported vehicle checkout, handling tests, and flight operations.

quadrants.title: Christina Koch
quadrants.body: Mission Specialist. Supported systems evaluation and science operations.

quadrants.title: Jeremy Hansen
quadrants.body: Mission Specialist. Represented the Canadian Space Agency on the crew.

slides.title: NASA's priorities were operational
slides.subtitle: The mission priorities document focused on what must work before lunar surface missions

cards.title: Crew Sustainment
cards.body: Demonstrate that systems and teams can sustain astronauts through flight and return.

cards.title: Systems and Ops
cards.body: Validate ground, launch, flight, and recovery operations essential to a crewed lunar campaign.

cards.title: Hardware and Data
cards.body: Recover flight hardware and data to assess performance before later missions.

slides.footer: NASA Artemis II Priorities and Objectives

slides.title: The mission ran exactly where NASA needed it
slides.subtitle: A short but high-consequence test sequence from launch to recovery

milestones.label: Apr 1
milestones.title: Launch
milestones.body: Orion launched from Kennedy at 6:35 p.m. EDT.

milestones.label: Day 2
milestones.title: Departure Burn
milestones.body: After initial checkout, Orion fired its main engine and set course for the Moon.

milestones.label: Apr 6
milestones.title: Lunar Flyby
milestones.body: The crew passed 4,067 miles above the lunar surface and captured more than 7,000 images.

milestones.label: Apr 10
milestones.title: Splashdown
milestones.body: Orion splashed down off California at 5:07 p.m. PDT.

slides.title: Artemis II materially derisks Artemis III
slides.subtitle: Flight results directly support the next mission's integrated landing architecture
bullets: NASA validated Orion life support, critical crew survival systems, and extended-duration crew support operations.
The crew performed manual piloting and proximity operations that generate data for later rendezvous and docking work.
Reentry, parachute deployment, splashdown, and crew recovery all closed the end-to-end test loop.
The mission strengthened confidence in the vehicle, the teams, and the international operating model.
highlights: Focus now shifts to Artemis III integration.
The mission provided real flight data, not just ground-model assumptions.
Human lunar return now rests on a proven crewed transport stack.
slides.footer: NASA release and mission objectives, status as of April 13, 2026

slides.title: Artemis II mission profile in one sourced view
slides.subtitle: Elapsed day from launch to splashdown, paired with post-flight mission facts
slides.message: The flight was short by design, but it covered the checkpoints Artemis III depends on.
